caveats: a: Occupation evidence is for the three-digit fabricated-metal sector, not 332119.
a: No source measures current AI adoption or task exposure in stamping plants.
rho: This is bounded judgment rather than an observed five-year adoption rate.
rho: Conventional press automation is excluded unless AI is the operative substitution mechanism.
e: The provided n relies on receipts and a machinery-industry margin bridge rather than observed EBITDA.
e: The code combines packaging closures and diverse unfinished stampings with different commercial structures.
s5: No comprehensive six-digit control-transfer series was found.
s5: Asset auctions, customer-program transfers, and internal reorganizations must be excluded.
q: No source directly measures retention of AI-derived benefit.
q: Commodity crowns and high-volume stampings may face more pass-through than specialty low-volume parts.
d5: EPA packaging data cover steel containers broadly, not metal closures or all 332119 products, and are historical rather than a forecast.
d5: No complete constant-price industry demand forecast was found.
o: Domestic operators can be displaced even when US end demand persists.
o: Material or packaging elimination belongs in d5; o addresses who supplies remaining physical quantity.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring releases and production programs priced per part, thousand units, pound, lot, or program, commonly supported by customer-specific dies, specifications, quality documentation, and metal-price adjustments.
lens.excluded_activities: Automotive stampings, metal cans and finished containers, coins, captive internal stamping lines, manufacturers classified by a further-fabricated finished product, pure distributors, non-control financing vehicles, and operations that cannot transfer independently of the owner.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly stamping metal crowns, bottle caps, canning lids and rings, other unfinished non-automotive metal stampings, and spun unfinished metal products for external packaging, appliance, electrical, industrial, consumer-product, and equipment customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures all NAICS 332 fabricated metal manufacturing, not wage-weighted AI task exposure in 332119. In May 2023, production occupations were 58.31% of sector employment; cutting, punching, and press machine operators alone were 57,710 workers or 4.01%, while metal/plastic workers, assemblers, welders, CNC workers, inspectors, maintenance, and material-moving roles make the broader physical core. I assign low exposure to die setup, coil handling, press operation, spinning, deburring, maintenance, packing, and physical inspection, but higher exposure to RFQ/specification review, quoting, die and process documentation, scheduling, purchasing, quality records, and customer service. Stamping firms may have more press labor than the broad sector, so the proxy may overstate exposure.
caveats: Occupation evidence is for the three-digit fabricated-metal sector, not 332119.
No source measures current AI adoption or task exposure in stamping plants.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task-time study across 15-20 independent closure and non-automotive stamping plants, documenting current automation and labor by commercial, engineering, tooling, production, quality, logistics, and administration, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: High-volume stamping and closure production are physical, safety-sensitive processes, but repeat customer programs generate structured workflows around drawings, dies, change notices, prices, schedules, certificates, inspection, and corrective actions that AI can partially automate.

caveats: EPA packaging data cover steel containers broadly, not metal closures or all 332119 products, and are historical rather than a forecast.
No complete constant-price industry demand forecast was found.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price Census shipments by product class, physical closure and stamped-part volumes, customer program forecasts, beverage and food packaging outlooks, and appliance/equipment production schedules would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Closures and unfinished stampings serve recurring physical packaging and manufactured-product demand. Beverage, food, home canning, appliance, electrical, and industrial uses provide a broad floor, while lightweighting, plastic or composite substitution, refill/reuse, imports, customer insourcing, and economic cycles constrain growth.

caveats: The provided n relies on receipts and a machinery-industry margin bridge rather than observed EBITDA.
The code combines packaging closures and diverse unfinished stampings with different commercial structures.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm review of the 473 dataset-estimated candidates covering ownership, external repeat revenue, custom-versus-proprietary mix, normalized EBITDA, tooling rights, customer concentration, equipment, and owner dependence would replace this estimate.
e.rationale: Most independent stamping firms repeatedly supply external customer programs, but crown and closure producers can be proprietary-scale manufacturers rather than outsourced job shops. Captive operations, firms outside the true EBITDA band, customer concentration, weak die ownership or transfer rights, obsolete equipment, and owner-dependent engineering or sales reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 332119 ($3,176,334,916) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 332119 ($14,691,415,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $312,810 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332119); class <5: 173 firms, avg revenue/firm $862,017 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $169,128 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 166 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,449,928 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $284,476 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 182 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,117,764 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $611,705 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 355 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,383,048 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,429,554 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 118 firms, avg revenue/firm $42,593,517 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,356,848 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 50 firms, avg revenue/firm $86,242,960 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $16,920,869 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 473

caveats: Domestic operators can be displaced even when US end demand persists.
Material or packaging elimination belongs in d5; o addresses who supplies remaining physical quantity.
o.change_evidence: Customer sourcing interviews and lost-order data quantifying insourcing, plastic/composite substitution, additive or alternate forming, offshore supply, reusable-packaging redesign, and finished-product integration would materially change this value.
o.rationale: Remaining demand is for physical closures or stamped/spun parts and requires accountable die and material control, press or spinning operation, finishing, inspection, and delivery. Software cannot supply the output; customer insourcing, alternate materials, additive processes, or offshore sourcing shift or remove the external operator.

caveats: No source directly measures retention of AI-derived benefit.
Commodity crowns and high-volume stampings may face more pass-through than specialty low-volume parts.
q.change_evidence: Program contracts, die ownership, metal pass-through and productivity clauses, annual cost-down terms, quote and resourcing histories, customer concentration, and margin bridges after automation would establish actual retention.
q.rationale: Customer-specific dies, qualifications, high-speed process know-how, quality history, and delivery performance create switching friction. Metal-index mechanisms, annual cost-down demands, package and component rebids, open-book programs, and large customer procurement share recurring productivity gains.

caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than an observed five-year adoption rate.
Conventional press automation is excluded unless AI is the operative substitution mechanism.
rho.change_evidence: Plant deployments reporting sustained labor hours, quote time, engineering-change errors, schedule adherence, scrap, quality escapes, false alarms, user adoption, and customer acceptance would materially update the range.
rho.rationale: Assisted estimating, drawing and revision comparison, schedule and material recommendations, certificate handling, inspection-data review, and corrective-action drafting are implementable within five years. Legacy ERP, weak data, customer approvals, tool and process liability, food-contact or packaging specifications, cyber requirements, and physical verification limit realization.

caveats: No comprehensive six-digit control-transfer series was found.
Asset auctions, customer-program transfers, and internal reorganizations must be excluded.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-matched ten-year transaction set from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, and metal-stamping advisers, paired with owner-age and succession-plan data for eligible firms, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: A sizable mature private supplier base should generate retirement, strategic consolidation, and sponsor transactions, but customer concentration, tooling and equipment diligence, family succession, cyclicality, and closures or asset-only sales reduce completed qualifying control transfers.

principal_driver: Recurring customer programs create repeat quote, drawing, change-control, planning, inspection, and quality workflows with supplier switching friction.
principal_weakness: Most labor is physical, and high-volume customers can force productivity gains into price through cost downs and rebids.

run_meta.title: Metal Crown, Closure, and Other Metal Stamping (except Automotive)

sources.supports: BLS reports 1,439,340 NAICS 332 workers in May 2023, including 57,710 cutting, punching, and press machine operators (4.01%), 46,870 inspectors/testers (3.26%), and a broad production majority.
sources.title: Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates

sources.supports: Census defines the industry as stamping metal crowns and closures such as bottle caps and canning lids/rings and manufacturing other unfinished non-automotive, non-can, non-coin metal stampings and spun products.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 332119 Metal Crown, Closure, and Other Metal Stamping (except Automotive)

sources.supports: DOE describes stamping presses and dies as tools that shape and cut sheet metal into finished parts and lists punching, blanking, embossing, bending, flanging, and coining, supporting the physical-process assessment.
sources.title: DOE Module 3C: Sheet Metal Stamping

sources.supports: EPA reports 2.2 million tons of steel food cans, other cans, and steel packaging generated in 2018 and 1.6 million tons recycled, a 73.8% recycling rate; used as broad packaging context, not a 332119 demand measure.
sources.title: Containers and Packaging: Product-Specific Data

title: Metal Crown, Closure, and Other Metal Stamping (except Automotive)
